entries.caseReference: AIR 2001 SC 1158= 2001(3) SCC 1- Bipin Shantilal Panchal V. State of Gujrat
entries.issue: Can a trial court defer the decision on the admissibility of objected oral or documentary evidence, noting the objection and tentatively marking the evidence, until the final judgment stage?
entries.holding: Yes, the trial court can make a note of any objection raised during the evidence-taking stage regarding the admissibility of material or oral evidence, and tentatively mark the objected document as an exhibit (or record the objected part of oral evidence). The decision on such objections can be deferred to the final judgment. If the court finds the objection sustainable at the final stage, it can exclude such evidence from consideration. This procedure aims to prevent delays in trial proceedings. However, objections relating to deficiency of stamp duty must be decided immediately.
advocateNotes: Advise the court to record objections contemporaneously and seek tentative exhibit marking for documents, clarifying that final admissibility will be determined at judgment to ensure trial efficiency.

Be prepared to argue for immediate ruling on objections concerning stamp duty or fundamental inadmissibility, as the Bipin Panchal rule has specific exceptions and has been clarified by subsequent judgments.

Emphasize that tentative marking does not equate to proof; ensure all tentatively marked documents are formally proved as per the Indian Evidence Act during the trial to establish their evidentiary value.
relatedDetails: Distinction between 'Admissibility' and 'Proof' of documents in Indian evidence law.

Impact of *State (through Special Cell), New Delhi v. Navjot Sandhu & Ors.* and *In Re: To Issue Certain Guidelines Regarding Inadequacies and Deficiencies in Criminal Trials (2021)* on the *Bipin Panchal* principle, emphasizing immediate decision on objections in certain contexts.
Mandatory immediate decision on objections relating to deficiency of stamp duty, as explicitly carved out by the Supreme Court.

entries.caseReference: (2022)10 SCC 51- Satender Kumar Antil V. CBI
entries.issue: What are the mandatory conditions and procedural safeguards under Sections 41 and 41A CrPC for arrest and bail, particularly for offences punishable with imprisonment up to seven years, and the procedure for accused not arrested during investigation upon filing of chargesheet?
entries.holding: The Supreme Court laid down comprehensive guidelines for arrest and bail, categorizing offences into four types (A, B, C, D). For Category A offences (punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years, not falling in B & D), if the accused was not arrested during investigation and cooperated, they should ordinarily be granted bail upon appearance before the Magistrate post-chargesheet, without being taken into custody. The Court emphasized that Sections 41 and 41A CrPC are facets of Article 21 of the Constitution, mandating recording of reasons for arrest/non-arrest and strict compliance with Section 41A notice. Non-compliance with these provisions entitles the accused to bail. The judgment also clarified that Section 170 CrPC does not mandate the arrest of an accused at the time of filing the chargesheet if custodial interrogation is not required.
advocateNotes: Insist on strict compliance with Section 41/41A CrPC, including recorded reasons for arrest/non-arrest, and challenge any mechanical arrest or remand.

For Category A offences (up to 7 years imprisonment), argue for bail as a matter of course if the accused cooperated and was not arrested during investigation, citing the 'bail is the rule' principle.
Challenge any attempt to take an accused into custody merely upon filing of a chargesheet if they were not arrested during investigation and cooperated.
relatedDetails: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar (2014) (precedent on Section 41/41A CrPC).

Magistrate's duty to scrutinize reasons for arrest/detention.

entries.caseReference: (2014) 8 SCC 273- Arnesh Kumar V. State of Bihar
entries.issue: Whether police officers can make routine arrests for cognizable and non-bailable offences, particularly those punishable with imprisonment up to seven years, without satisfying the conditions and recording reasons as mandated by Section 41 Cr.P.C., and the role of magistrates in authorizing such detention.
entries.holding: The Supreme Court held that arrest should not be a routine or automatic exercise merely because an offence is cognizable and non-bailable, especially for offences punishable with imprisonment up to seven years. Police officers are mandated to record in writing the reasons for arrest, satisfying specific conditions under Section 41(1)(b)(ii) Cr.P.C. (e.g., to prevent further offence, for proper investigation, to prevent tampering with evidence, or to secure presence in court). If arrest is not necessary, a notice under Section 41A Cr.P.C. must be issued. Magistrates, before authorizing detention under Section 167 Cr.P.C., must scrutinize the police report and recorded reasons, ensuring compliance with Section 41 Cr.P.C., and record their own satisfaction. Non-compliance by police officers may lead to departmental action and contempt of court, while magistrates authorizing detention mechanically may face departmental action by the High Court.
advocateNotes: Insist on the police officer's 'checklist' and recorded reasons for arrest, challenging any mechanical arrest as a violation of Arnesh Kumar guidelines.
Demand issuance of a Section 41A Cr.P.C. notice for offences punishable up to 7 years, emphasizing that arrest is an exception, not the rule.
Challenge judicial remand orders if the Magistrate has not recorded independent satisfaction regarding the necessity of arrest and detention, beyond mere police ipse dixit.

entries.caseReference: (2021)2 SCC 427- Arnab Manoranjan Goswami V. State of Maharashtra
entries.issue: Whether High Courts, in exercising powers under Article 226/Section 482 CrPC, abdicate their constitutional duty by refusing interim bail or failing to conduct a prima facie evaluation of an FIR that does not disclose a cognizable offence, thereby infringing personal liberty under Article 21.
entries.holding: The Supreme Court held that High Courts must act as the 'first line of defence' for citizens' liberty and not abdicate their constitutional duty by routinely denying interim bail or postponing petitions for quashing FIRs, especially when a prima facie case is not made out. It reiterated that 'bail is the rule, jail is the exception' and emphasized the need for courts to evaluate the FIR's contents to determine if the ingredients of the alleged offence are disclosed. The Court granted interim bail and quashed the FIR, finding no prima facie case for abetment of suicide under Section 306 IPC.

entries.caseReference: (2007) 4 SCC 511= 2007(3) WBLR 525- Samar Ghosh V. Jaya Ghosh
entries.issue: What constitutes 'mental cruelty' as a ground for divorce under the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, and how should courts determine its presence, considering its subjective, evolving nature, and the cumulative impact of specific conduct?
entries.holding: The Supreme Court held that 'mental cruelty' cannot be exhaustively defined, as it is subjective and depends on the parties' background, sensitivity, social status, and cultural context. The Court provided illustrative, non-exhaustive instances of conduct that could amount to mental cruelty, such as unilateral decisions not to have children, persistent refusal of cohabitation, neglect during illness, and humiliating treatment. The determination requires a comprehensive appraisal of the entire matrimonial life, focusing on the cumulative effect of actions that cause acute mental pain, agony, and suffering, making it impossible for the parties to reasonably live together. The High Court's reversal of the trial court's divorce decree was set aside, and the trial court's judgment granting divorce was restored.
advocateNotes: Demonstrate sustained conduct and contextual impact, not isolated incidents, by presenting a pattern of behavior.
Frame cruelty using contemporary social context and marital realities, aligning arguments with evolving societal norms.
Emphasize the cumulative effect of actions and link specific conduct to the petitioner's mental suffering, utilizing the illustrative instances from the judgment as a guide.
relatedDetails: Irretrievable breakdown of marriage (as a factor, though not a statutory ground).

entries.caseReference: AIR 2020 SC 111- Munish Kakkar V. Nidhi Kakkar
entries.issue: Can the Supreme Court grant divorce on the ground of irretrievable breakdown of marriage, even if not explicitly provided by statute, by exercising its extraordinary powers under Article 142 of the Constitution?
entries.holding: Yes, the Supreme Court, exercising its extraordinary powers under Article 142 of the Constitution, can grant a decree of divorce on the ground of irretrievable breakdown of marriage. This is permissible particularly in cases where the parties have been in protracted litigation for decades, the marriage has become a 'dead letter' or 'emotionally dead', and its continuation causes further emotional trauma and disturbance to both parties, even if statutory grounds for divorce under the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 are not strictly met. The Court aims to do 'complete justice' by allowing parties to move on with their lives when reconciliation is impossible.
entries.subCategory: Irretrievable Breakdown of Marriage & Supreme Court's Article 142 Powers
advocateNotes: Emphasize the long duration of separation, failed reconciliation attempts, and the complete absence of a matrimonial bond to establish 'irretrievable breakdown' for Article 142 intervention.
Highlight that while Article 142 provides discretionary power, it is exercised with great caution in 'rare cases' where the marriage is 'totally unworkable, emotionally dead, beyond salvage'.
Advise clients that while mutual consent strengthens the case, the Supreme Court can grant divorce on this ground even if one party opposes it, provided the marriage is beyond repair.

entries.caseReference: AIR 1985 SC 945 - Mohammad Ahmed Khan V. Shah Bano Begum
entries.issue: Does a divorced Muslim woman have the right to claim maintenance under Section 125 of the Criminal Procedure Code, 1973, even after the 'iddat' period, if she is unable to maintain herself, notwithstanding provisions of Muslim personal law?
entries.holding: The Supreme Court held that Section 125 Cr.P.C. is a secular provision applicable to all citizens, including Muslim women, to prevent destitution and vagrancy. A divorced Muslim wife is entitled to maintenance under Section 125 Cr.P.C. if she is unable to maintain herself, and this liability of the husband extends beyond the 'iddat' period. The Court clarified that there is no conflict between Section 125 Cr.P.C. and Muslim personal law regarding a husband's obligation to provide maintenance to a divorced wife, interpreting the Quran to support this obligation. In case of any perceived conflict, Section 125 Cr.P.C., being a secular social justice law, overrides personal law. The maintenance amount should be 'fair and reasonable' considering the husband's means and the wife's needs.
entries.subCategory: Maintenance Rights of Divorced Muslim Women
statuteTags: Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986
advocateNotes: When arguing for maintenance under Section 125 CrPC for a Muslim woman, emphasize the secular and welfare nature of the provision, asserting its applicability irrespective of personal law, especially where the woman is unable to maintain herself.
Carefully quantify the 'fair and reasonable provision' by presenting detailed evidence of the husband's financial capacity and the wife's actual needs, extending beyond the 'iddat' period.

Be prepared to address the interplay with the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986, by arguing that rights under the 1986 Act are in addition to, and not in derogation of, Section 125 CrPC, as affirmed by subsequent judgments.

entries.caseReference: AIR 2001 SC 3958 - Daniel Latifi v. Union of India
entries.issue: Whether the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986 (MWPRDA) is constitutionally valid and if a divorced Muslim woman's right to maintenance under Section 125 CrPC is curtailed by the MWPRDA, particularly regarding maintenance beyond the 'iddat' period.
entries.holding: The Supreme Court upheld the constitutional validity of the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986, by interpreting it purposively. It ruled that a Muslim husband is liable to make a 'reasonable and fair provision' for his divorced wife, which extends beyond the 'iddat' period and covers her future needs, including maintenance for her entire life. This provision, while required to be made within the 'iddat' period, is not limited in its scope to that period. This interpretation harmonized the MWPRDA with Section 125 CrPC and Articles 14, 15, and 21 of the Constitution, ensuring that divorced Muslim women are not left destitute.
entries.subCategory: Muslim Women Maintenance Rights & Constitutional Validity of MWPRDA
statuteTags: Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986
advocateNotes: Plead a harmonized and purposive reading of Section 125 CrPC and the MWPRDA, emphasizing the husband's continuing liability for 'reasonable and fair provision' beyond the 'iddat' period.
Quantify 'fair and reasonable provision' with robust evidentiary support, demonstrating the divorced wife's lifetime needs and the husband's financial capacity.
Highlight the constitutional imperative (Articles 14, 15, 21) to prevent destitution and ensure gender justice, even when arguing under special personal laws.

entries.caseReference: 2019(3) Divorce and Matrimonial Case 789- Jubair Ahmed V. Ishrat Bano
entries.issue: Can a divorced Muslim woman claim ongoing maintenance under Section 125 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, even if her earlier claim under the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986, was rejected, and does such a rejection operate as res judicata?
entries.holding: Yes, a divorced Muslim woman is entitled to claim maintenance under Section 125 CrPC, as this provision is secular and aims to prevent destitution, applying to all women irrespective of religion. The Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act, 1986 (MWPRDA) provides an additional, not derogating, remedy. The Supreme Court, in *Danial Latifi v. Union of India* (2001) and *Shabana Bano v. Imran Khan* (2010), clarified that the husband's liability under Section 3(1)(a) of MWPRDA to make a 'reasonable and fair provision and maintenance' extends beyond the iddat period for the divorced wife's lifetime or until remarriage. Therefore, an earlier rejection of a claim under MWPRDA does not bar a subsequent application under Section 125 CrPC, and the principle of res judicata does not apply, as the remedies are distinct and concurrent.
entries.subCategory: Maintenance Rights of Divorced Muslim Women
advocateNotes: Emphasize the secular nature of Section 125 CrPC and its objective to prevent vagrancy, citing Supreme Court precedents like *Shabana Bano* to establish concurrent remedies.
Argue that the 'reasonable and fair provision' under MWPRDA is a one-time settlement, distinct from the ongoing monthly maintenance under Section 125 CrPC, thus avoiding res judicata.
Ensure comprehensive documentation of the wife's inability to maintain herself and the husband's means, as these are crucial for securing maintenance under Section 125 CrPC.

entries.caseReference: AIR 2022 SC 2175= 2022(7) SCC 147= 2022(3) CHN 108- Abdul Motin Mallick V. Subrata Bhattacharjee (Bannerjee) & Ors.
entries.issue: Is the deposit of the entire sale consideration plus 10% mandatory for the maintainability of a pre-emption application under Section 8 of the West Bengal Land Reforms Act, 1955?
entries.holding: The Supreme Court held that the deposit of the entire sale consideration along with an additional 10% of that consideration is a mandatory condition precedent for the maintainability of a pre-emption application under Section 8 of the West Bengal Land Reforms Act, 1955. Failure to make this deposit concurrently with the filing of the application renders the application non-maintainable and liable to be dismissed, affirming the strict interpretation of the statutory requirement.
advocateNotes: Ensure concurrent deposit of the full sale consideration plus 10% with the pre-emption application; non-compliance is fatal and cannot be cured later.
Strictly adhere to all statutory timelines and procedural conditions for pre-emption under the WBLR Act, as the right is considered 'weak' and requires complete compliance.

entries.caseReference: AIR 2011 SC 697 State of UP V. Chhoteylal
entries.issue: Can the absence of immediate alarm or physical resistance by a prosecutrix in a kidnapping and rape case lead to an inference of willing accompaniment or consent, especially when under threat?
entries.holding: No, the absence of alarm by the prosecutrix at public places cannot automatically lead to an inference of willing accompaniment or consent. The Supreme Court held that when a victim is kidnapped by adult males, one of whom wields a firearm and issues threats, her decision not to raise an alarm is a sensible survival response to avoid endangering her life. The Court emphasized that victim testimony, if reliable, can be sufficient for conviction, and her evidence should not be viewed with suspicion or subjected to stereotypical expectations of how a victim 'should' react. The High Court's acquittal based on such stereotypical grounds was overturned.
advocateNotes: Highlight the prosecutrix's testimony as primary evidence
emphasize its inherent reliability
noting that corroboration is not a legal mandate but strengthens the case.
Vigorously challenge defense arguments predicated on 'ideal victim' stereotypes
such as absence of immediate resistance

entries.caseReference: AIR 2006 SC 1267- Dinesh @ Buddha V. State of Rajasthan
entries.issue: Should the identity of a sexual assault victim be disclosed in court judgments, particularly in light of Section 228A IPC's social object, even if the penal provision doesn't directly apply to higher court publications?
entries.holding: The Supreme Court deprecated the practice of disclosing the identity of sexual assault victims in judgments. While Section 228A IPC penalizes the printing or publication of victim identity by others, its explanation within sub-section (3) clarifies that the printing or publication of a judgment of any High Court or the Supreme Court does not amount to an offence under this section. However, the Court emphasized that the underlying social object of Section 228A—to prevent social victimization and protect the victim's dignity and privacy—mandates that courts, as a matter of judicial practice and prudence, should refrain from disclosing the victim's name or any identifying particulars in their judgments. This directive reinforces the judiciary's role in safeguarding the victim's fundamental rights, including the right to dignity under Article 21 of the Constitution.
entries.subCategory: Victim Anonymity & Dignity in Sexual Offence Cases
advocateNotes: Always anonymize victim details (e.g., 'victim,' 'prosecutrix,' 'X') in all court filings and oral submissions to align with judicial directives and protect privacy.

entries.caseReference: AIR 2009 SC 1010- (State of HP V. Prem Singh)
entries.issue: Whether delay in lodging a First Information Report (FIR) in sexual assault cases, particularly given the unique societal and psychological factors affecting victims, can be a sole ground to discredit the prosecution's case.
entries.holding: The Supreme Court consistently holds that delay in lodging an FIR in sexual assault cases cannot be equated with other offenses due to the unique trauma, fear of social stigma, and family honor concerns faced by victims. Such delay, if satisfactorily explained, should not be a ritualistic formula to discard the prosecution's case. In *State of HP v. Prem Singh*, while the evidence did not meet the statutory ingredients for rape under Section 375 IPC, the accused was convicted for outraging modesty under Section 354 IPC (assault or criminal force with intent to outrage modesty) and criminal intimidation under Section 506 IPC (threatening injury to person, reputation, or property with intent to cause alarm). The Court emphasized a realistic, rather than theoretical, approach to such delays.
advocateNotes: Frame dignity and bodily autonomy as central judicial values, emphasizing the victim's perspective in sexual assault cases.
Challenge victim-shaming narratives and arguments based on 'normal' behavior with settled jurisprudence on victim vulnerability and societal pressures.
Present a detailed and convincing explanation for any delay in FIR, linking it to the specific trauma, fear, and social stigma experienced by the prosecutrix.
relatedDetails: Victim-centric approach in sexual assault cases
Corroboration of victim testimony (not mandatory, but prudential)

entries.caseReference: AIR 1996 SC 309- [Rupan Deol Bajaj V. Kanwar Pal Singh Gill (K.P.S Gill)]
entries.issue: What constitutes 'outraging the modesty of a woman' under Sections 354 and 509 of the Indian Penal Code, and the appropriate test for determining such an offense, particularly when the act lacks explicit 'sexual overtones'?
entries.holding: The Supreme Court established that the ultimate test for ascertaining whether modesty has been outraged under Sections 354 and 509 IPC is whether the offender's action is capable of shocking a woman's sense of decency. The Court clarified that 'modesty' refers to "womanly propriety of behavior; scrupulous chastity of thought, speech and conduct". An act like slapping a woman on her posterior in a public gathering constitutes outraging her modesty, as it is an affront to feminine decency and dignity, irrespective of whether it carries explicit "sexual overtones". This ruling overturned the High Court's decision to quash the FIR, emphasizing that such acts are not trivial and cannot be dismissed under Section 95 IPC. The judgment underscored the importance of intent or knowledge for culpability under Section 354 IPC.
advocateNotes: Frame dignity and bodily autonomy as central judicial values, emphasizing that 'modesty' is inherent to a woman's personhood, not her attire or conduct.
Challenge victim-shaming narratives by citing this precedent, which establishes that the offense is determined by the impact on the victim's sense of decency, not societal judgments of her 'modesty'.
Highlight that the absence of explicit "sexual overtones" does not negate the offense under Section 354 IPC, focusing on the intent or knowledge of outraging modesty.
relatedDetails: Test of Modesty: The "shocking sense of decency" test established by the Supreme Court.
Quashing of FIRs: Principles governing the exercise of powers under Section 482 CrPC, particularly in cases involving offenses against women.
Dignity and Bodily Integrity: The implicit recognition of a woman's fundamental right to dignity and bodily integrity, predating explicit sexual harassment laws.

entries.caseReference: 2015 CriLJ 3976 (State of MP V. Madan Lal)
entries.issue: Can the offence of rape or attempt to rape, being non-compoundable under Section 320 of the Criminal Procedure Code, 1973, be permitted to be compounded by a court, even through the exercise of inherent powers?
entries.holding: The Supreme Court unequivocally held that the offence of rape or attempt to rape is a heinous crime against the body of a woman and society at large, and therefore, it is not compoundable under Section 320 of the CrPC. The Court emphasized that allowing a compromise in such grave matters would be against public policy and would undermine the administration of criminal justice. While High Courts possess inherent powers under Section 482 CrPC to quash proceedings in certain non-compoundable offences, this power cannot be invoked to permit compounding of serious offences like rape, which involve mental depravity and have a profound impact beyond the individual victim. The High Court's decision to convert the conviction from attempt to rape (Section 376(2)(f) read with Section 511 IPC) to Section 354 IPC and allow a compromise was deemed legally unsustainable.
entries.subCategory: Non-Compoundability of Sexual Offences & Judicial Discretion
advocateNotes: Strongly argue against any attempt to compound sexual offences, emphasizing their non-compoundable nature and societal impact, citing this judgment.
Highlight the limitations on inherent powers (Section 482 CrPC) in heinous crimes like rape, even if parties express a desire for compromise.
Prepare to distinguish cases where inherent powers are used for quashing (e.g., matrimonial disputes with civil character) from grave offences like rape where such discretion is impermissible.

entries.holding: Police arresting a person in a criminal offence- he was tortured and he humiliated while in custody- Torture is not merely physical but may even consist of mental. entries.caseReference: AIR 1964 (SC) 1254 (1256)- S.M. Karim V. Mst Bibi Sakina
entries.issue: On Adverse Possession
entries.holding: Adverse possession must be adequate in continuity, in publicity and extent and a plea is required at least to show when possession becomes adverse, so that the starting point of limitation against the party affected can be found. entries.caseReference: 2014(8) Supreme Court Cases 340 [Chandra Prakash v. State of Rajasthan (SC)]
entries.issue: Criminal Procedure Code, Sections 306 and 309 - Approver's evidence - Principles which relate to the acceptance of the evidence of an approver
entries.holding: Principles which relate to the acceptance of the evidence of an approver summed up :- (i) An approver is a most unworthy friend, if at all, and he, having bargained for his immunity, must prove his worthiness for credibility in court. (ii) Evidence of the approver is always to be viewed with suspicion especially when it is seriously suspected that he is suppressing some material facts. (iii) The evidence of the approver implicating several accused persons in the commission of the offence must not only be corroborated generally but also qua each accused but that does not mean that there should be independent corroboration of every particular circumstance from an independent source. (iv) Conviction could only be based on the testimony of the accomplice if it is thought reliable as a whole and if it is corroborated by independent evidence either direct or circumstantial, connecting the accused with the crime. (v) Evidence of an approver and the corroborating pieces of evidence should be treated in two different compartments, that is to say, the court shall first have to consider the evidence of the approver dehors the corroborated pieces of evidence and reject it if it comes to the conclusion that his evidence is unreliable; but if it comes to the conclusion that it is reliable, then it will have to consider whether that evidence is corroborated by any other evidence. (vi) The story given by an approver so far as the accused on trial is concerned, must implicate him in such a manner as to give rise to a conclusion of guilt beyond reasonable doubt. (vii) The nature and extent of the corroboration may depend upon the facts of each case and the corroboration need not be of any direct evidence that the accused committed the crime - The corroboration even by circumstantial evidence may be sufficient.
advocateNotes: Ensure every material incriminating circumstance is put to the accused.

entries.caseReference: 1999(2) SCC 228 = AIR 1999 Supreme Court 1149 (Githa Hariharan v. Reserve Bank of India)
entries.issue: Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956, Sections 4 and 6(a) - Guardian and Wards Act, 1890, Section 19(b) Both the father and mother are the natural guardian
entries.holding: Natural Guardians - Constitution of India, Articles 14 and 16 - Whether the preferential right given to the husband for the custody of minor children is discriminatory against the rights of the wife ? - The phrase, "the father, and after him, the mother," as found in Section 6(a) of 1956 Act, on cursory reading, gives an impression that the mother can be considered to be natural guardian of the minor only after the life time of the father - It is not so - The Parliament never intended to transgress the constitutional limits or ignore the fundamental rights guaranteed by the Constitution which prohibits discrimination on grounds of sex - The word 'after' need not necessarily mean 'after the life time' - In the context in which it appears in Section 6(a) of 1956 Act, it means 'in the absence of' i.e. absence from the care of the minor's property or person for any reason whatever including his physical or mental incapacity and the mother being recognised natural guardian, can act validly on behalf of the minor as the guardian - Such an interpretation will be the natural outcome of harmonious construction of Sections 4 and 6 of HMG Act without causing any violence to the language of Section 6(a) - The paramount consideration is the welfare of the minor in the widest sense - If necessary the father can be replaced by the mother or any other suitable person by an order of the court.
advocateNotes: Anchor relief in Article 21 fairness and procedural reasonableness.

entries.holding: ` While appreciating the evidence of a witness, the Court has to assess whether read as a whole, it is truthful. entries.caseReference: AIR 2011 SC 280 (Brahm Swaroop and Anr v. State of U.P)
entries.issue: Interested Witnesses - Merely because the witnesses were closely related to the deceased persons, their testimonies cannot be discarded
entries.holding: Merely because the witnesses were closely related to the deceased persons, their testimonies cannot be discarded. Their relationship to one of the parties is not a factor that effects the credibility of a witness, more so, a relation would not conceal the actual culprit and make allegations against an innocent person. A party has to lay down a factual foundation and prove by leading impeccable evidence in respect of its false implication. However, in such cases, the Court has to adopt a careful approach and analyse the evidence to find out whether it is cogent and credible evidence.

entries.caseReference: AIR 1965 SC 202 (Masati vs. State of Uttar Pradesh)
entries.issue: Interested Witnesses - Whether or not there are discrepancies in the evidence; whether or not evidence strikes the Court as genuine; whether or not the story disclosed by the evidence is probable, are all matters which must be taken into account. But, it would be unreasonable to contend that evidence given by witnesses should be discarded only on the ground that it is evidence of partisan or interested witnesses.
entries.holding: There is no doubt that when a criminal Court has to appreciate evidence given by witnesses who are partisan or interested, it has to be very careful in weighing such evidence. Whether or not there are discrepancies in the evidence; whether or not evidence strikes the Court as genuine; whether or not the story disclosed by the evidence is probable, are all matters which must be taken into account. But, it would be unreasonable to contend that evidence given by witnesses should be discarded only on the ground that it is evidence of partisan or interested witnesses. Often enough, where factions prevail in villagers and murders are committed as a result of enmity between such factions, criminal Courts have to deal with evidence of a partisan type. The mechanical rejection of such evidence on the sole ground that it is partisan would invariably lead to failure of justice. No hard and fast rule can be laid down as to how much evidence should be appreciated. Judicial approach has to be cautious in dealing with such evidence; but the plea that such evidence should be rejected because it is partisan cannot be accepted as correct.
advocateNotes: Invite court to test consistency on material particulars, not minor contradictions.
Address related/interested witness concerns through corroborative circumstances.

entries.caseReference: AIR 2004 SC 1053 (Ramanand Yadav v. Prabhu Nath Jha and Ors)
entries.issue: Relatives or interested witnesses - If the relatives or interested witnesses are examined, the Court has a duty to analyse the evidence with deeper scrutiny

entries.caseReference: 2012 CriLJ 4174 (State Tr. P.S. Lodhi Colony New Delhi v. Sanjeev Nanda)
entries.issue: Evidence Act, 1872, S.3 - Appreciation of evidence - If a witness becomes hostile to subvert the judicial process, the Courts shall not stand as a mute spectator and every effort should be made to bring home the truth
entries.holding: Witnesses - Menance of witnesses turning hostile - Erodes criminal judicial system - Court should not be mute spectator but shall make every effort to bring home truth. Witness turning hostile is a major disturbing factor faced by the criminal Courts in India. Reasons are many for the witnesses turning hostile, but of late, especially in high profile cases, there is a regularity in the witnesses turning hostile, either due to monetary consideration or by other tempting offers which undermine the entire criminal justice system and people carry the impression that the mighty and powerful can always get away from the clutches of law thereby, eroding people's faith in the system. Courts, however, cannot shut their eyes to the reality. If a witness becomes hostile to subvert the judicial process, the Courts shall not stand as a mute spectator and every effort should be made to bring home the truth. Criminal judicial system cannot be overturned by those gullible witnesses who act under pressure, inducement or intimidation.

entries.caseReference: (2017) 11 SCC 535 (Shama v. State of Haryana)
entries.issue: Section 32, Evidence Act , 1872 - Law does not prescribe any format for recording dying declaration
entries.holding: Law does not prescribe any format for recording dying declaration; and secondly, it also does not prescribe any specific authority to record it unless any special law or rule is enacted to that effect. On the other hand, perfect working and neatly structured dying declaration at times brings about an adverse impression and creates suspicion in the mind of court, since dying declaration need not be drawn with mathematical precision. All that law requires is that declarant should be in a fit state of mind and be able to recollect the situation resulting in the available state of affairs in relation to the incident and the court should be satisfied that reliance ought to be placed thereon rather than distrust.

entries.caseReference: (1984) 4 SCC 116 : 1984 SCC (Cri) 486 (Sharad Birdhichand Sarda v. State of Maharashtra)
entries.issue: Section 32, Evidence Act , 1872 - The statement may be made before the cause of death has arisen, or before the deceased has any reason to anticipate being killed
entries.holding: The statement may be made before the cause of death has arisen, or before the deceased has any reason to anticipate being killed. But the necessary condition of admissibility under the section is that the circumstances must have some proximate relation to the actual occurrence. However, the test of proximity cannot be too literally construed and practically reduced to a cut-and-dried formula of universal application so as to be confined in a straitjacket. Distance of time would depend or vary with the circumstances of each case. Where death is a logical culmination of a long and continuous process, the statement regarding each step directly connected with the death would be admissible because the entire statement would have to be read as an organic whole not torn from the context, .

entries.caseReference: (2012) 7 SCC 569 : (2012) 3 SCC (Cri) 430 (Shudhakar v. State of M.P)
entries.issue: Section 32, Evidence Act , 1872 Dying declaration” is the last statement made by a person at a stage when he is in serious apprehension of his death and expects no chances of his survival.
entries.holding: “Dying declaration” is the last statement made by a person at a stage when he is in serious apprehension of his death and expects no chances of his survival. At such time, it is expected that a person will speak the truth and only the truth. Normally in such situations, courts attach intrinsic value of truthfulness to such statement-Once such statement has been made voluntarily, it is reliable and is not an attempt by deceased to cover up truth or falsely implicate a person, then courts can safely rely on such dying declaration and it can form the basis of conviction-More so, where version given by deceased as dying declaration is supported and corroborated by other prosecution evidence, there is no reason for courts to doubt truthfulness of such dying declaration

entries.caseReference: AIR 2005 SC 2191 (State of Nagaland v. Lipok AO)
entries.issue: Limitation Act, 1963 Section 5 - Condonation of delay in filing appeal
entries.holding: Law enunciated by Supreme Court summed up :- Whether or not there is sufficient cause for condonation of delay is a question of fact dependant upon the facts and circumstances of the particular case. 1979(4) SCC 365 relied. (2) Courts should be liberal in condoning delay - Expression "every day's delay must be explained" does not mean that a pedantic approach should be made. 1979(4) SCC 365 relied. (3) Unless want of bonafides of such inaction or negligence as would deprive a party of the protection of Section 5 is proved, the application must not be thrown out or any delay cannot be refused to be condoned. (4) Mistake of counsel by itself is always sufficient cause for condonation of delay - It is always a question whether the mistake was bonafide or was merely a device to cover an ulterior purpose. (5) If the refusal to condone the delay results in grave miscarriage of justice, it would be a ground to condone the delay. (6) Expression " sufficient cause " is adequately elastic to enable the court to apply the law in a meaningful manner which subserves the ends of justice - that being the life-purposes for the existence of the institution of courts. (7) Court should not adopt an injustice-oriented approach in rejecting the application for condonation of delay

entries.caseReference: AIR 2005 SC 2191 (State of Nagaland v. Lipok AO)
entries.issue: Limitation Act, 1963 Section 5 - Condonation of delay in filing appeal
entries.holding: Limitation Act, 1963 Section 5 Delay by Govt. in filing appeal - Law in regard to condonation of delay as enunciated by Supreme Court summed up :- (1) No separate standards to determine the cause laid by the State vis- a -vis private litigant could be laid to prove strict standards of sufficient cause - But at the same time, State cannot be put on same footing as an individual due to peculiar functioning of Governmental conditions - Individual is always quick in taking decision being legally injured person while State is an impersonal machinery working through its officers. 1996(3) SCC 132 and 1996(10) SCC 634 relied. (2) Fact that it was the State which was seeking condonation and not a private party was altogether irrelevant - There is no warrant for according step-motherly treatment when State is applicant. 1987(2) SCC 107 relied. (3) Law of limitation is same for Govt. and private individuals - But a somewhat different complexion is imparted to the matter where Government makes out a case where public interest was shown to have suffered owing to acts of fraud or bad faith on the part of its officers or agents and where the officers were clearly at cross-purposes with it. 1987 Supp SCC 339 relied. - It would be unrealistic to exclude from consideration those factors which are peculiar to functioning of Govt. - Govt. decisions a re proverbially slow - A certain amount of latitude is not impermissible. (4) Decisions are taken by officers/agencies proverbially at slow pace and encumbered process of pushing the files from table to table and keeping it on table for considerable time causing delay - intentional or otherwise - is a routine. Considerable delay of procedural red-tape in the process of their making decision is a common feature. Therefore, certain amount of latitude is not impermissible. (5) If appeals brought by State are lost for such default no person is individually affected but what in the ultimate analysis suffers, is public interest. The expression " sufficient cause " should, therefore, be considered with pragmatism in justice-oriented approach rather than the technical detection of sufficient cause for explaining every day's delay

entries.caseReference: AIR 2019 SC 2691 = (2019) 12 SCC 210 (M/s. SCG Contracts India Pvt. Ltd. v. K.S. Chamankar Infrastructure Pvt. Ltd.)

entries.caseReference: 100) AIR 1987 SC 1353 = 1987 (2) SCC 107 [Collector, Land Acquisition, Anantnag v. Mst. Katiji]
entries.issue: Constitution of India, Article 14 - Limitation Act, 1963, Section 5 - Condonation of delay - Sufficient cause
entries.holding: Courts directed to make justifiably liberal approach in the matter - Doctrine of equality - States cannot be given stepmotherly treatment. such a liberal approach is adopted on principle as it is realised that :- 1. Ordinarily a litigant does not stand to benefit by lodging an appeal late. 2. Refusing to condone delay can result in a meritorious matter being thrown out at the very threshold and cause of justice being defeated. As against this when delay is condoned the highest that can happen is that a cause would be decided on merits after hearing the parties. 3. "Every day's delay must be explained" does not mean that a pedantic approach should be made. Why not every hour's delay every second's delay? The doctrine must be applied in a rational common sense pragmatic manner. 4. When substantial justice and technical considerations are pitted against each other, cause of substantial justice deserves to be preferred for the other side cannot claim to have vested right in injustice being done because of a non-deliberate delay. 5. There is no presumption that delay is occasioned deliberately, or on account of culpable negligence, or on account of mala fides. A litigant does not stand to benefit by resorting to delay. In fact he runs a serious risk. 6. It must be grasped that judiciary is respected not on account of its power to legalise injustice on technical grounds but because it is capable of removing injustice and is expected to do so.

entries.caseReference: 107) 1990(1) Divorce and Matrimonial Cases 59 [Gitanjali Gajanan Tendulkar V. Gajanan Dinkar Tendulkar (Bombay)]
entries.issue: Son having attained the age of majority, he is entitled to maintenance
entries.holding: the law could be that inert. It is not so much of law as it is of life. The rule of law must run close to rule of life. The idea of granting maintenance is to see that the wife and children get a standard of living on par with the husband. Whether it is a full meal or half a meal, each one is entitled to a share. If a son reaches the age of eighteen, it does not mean that as from that date he has to starve. That is not the law. entries.issue: Sec 11, CPC - Res judicata and Order 2, Rule 2.-The doctrine of res judicata differs from the principle underlying Order 2, Rule 2

entries.issue: Criminal Procedure Code, 1973 Section 125 Muslim Women (Protection Of Rights On Divorce) Act, 1986 Section 5 Maintenance to divorced Muslim women
entries.holding: Magistrate has jurisdiction to grant maintenance under Section 125 otherwise than within parameters of Section 5 of Protection of Rights on Divorce Act- - Magistrate retains the power of granting maintenance under the Act and since the parameters for exercise of that power are the same as those contained in Section 125, Criminal Procedure Code, 1973 there is no ground to interfere with the orders of the Magistrate granting maintenance in favour of a divorced Muslim woman

entries.issue: Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2000, Section 41(5)
entries.holding: Adoption of a Muslim Child - Muslim Personal Law does not recognise adoption though it does not prohibit a childless couple from taking care and protecting a child with material and emotional support
advocateNotes: Prioritize welfare of child as paramount consideration across reliefs.

entries.caseReference: 126)2021(2) R.C.R. (Criminal) 300 = 2021 AIR (Supreme Court) 1381 (Krishna Lal Chawla v. State of U.P)
entries.issue: Constitution of India, 1950 Article 21 Criminal justice - Power of Trial Court protect rights of litigants
entries.holding: Trial courts and Magistrates have important role in curbing injustice - They are first lines of defence for both integrity of criminal justice system, and harassed and distraught litigant - Trial courts have power to not merely decide on acquittal or conviction of accused person after trial, but also duty to nip frivolous litigations in bud even before they reach stage of trial by discharging accused in fit cases - It would not only save judicial time that comes at cost of public money, but would also protect right to liberty that every person entitled - Trial Judges have as much, if not more, responsibility in safeguarding fundamental rights of citizens of India as highest court.
advocateNotes: Anchor relief in Article 21 fairness and procedural reasonableness.
Challenge arbitrary state action with rights-based proportionality analysis.

entries.caseReference: 127)2007(4) SCC 511 (Samar Ghosh v. Jaya Ghosh)
entries.issue: Concept of mental cruelty changes with change of time - What may be cruelty today may not be cruelty after passage of time.
entries.holding: What is cruelty in one case may not amount to cruelty in other case. The concept of cruelty differs from person to person depending upon his upbringing, level of sensitivity, educational, family and cultural background, financial position, social status, customs, traditions, religious beliefs, human values and their value system.
advocateNotes: Demonstrate sustained conduct and contextual impact, not isolated incidents.
Frame cruelty using contemporary social context and marital realities.

entries.caseReference: 130)AIR 1955 Madras 430 (T. Krishnaswamy Chetty v. C. Thanga-velu Chetty and others)
entries.issue: Civil Procedure Code, 1908, Order 40, Rule 1
entries.holding: Appointment of receiver - Words "Just and convenient" - Five principles which can be described : (1) The appointment of a receiver pending a suit is a matter resting in the discretion of the Court. The discretion is not arbitrary or absolute: it is a sound and judicial discretion, taking into account all the circumstances of the case, exercised for the purpose of permitting the ends of justice, and protecting the rights of all parties interested in the controversy and the subject-matter and based upon the fact that there is no other adequate remedy or means of accomplishing the desired objects of the judicial proceeding. (2) The Court should not appoint a receiver except upon proof by the plaintiff that prima facie he has very excellent chance of succeeding in the suit. (3) Not only must the plaintiff show a case of adverse and conflicting claims to property, but, he must show some emergency or danger or loss demanding immediate action and of his own right he must be reasonably clear and free from doubt. The element of danger is an important consideration. A Court will not act on possible danger only; the danger must be great and imminent demanding immediate relief. It has been truly said that a Court will never appoint a receiver merely on the ground that it will do no harm. (4) An order appointing a receiver will not be made where it has the effect of depriving a defendant of a 'de facto' possession since that might cause irreparable wrong. If the dispute is as to title only, the Court very reluctantly disturbs possession by receiver, but if the property is exposed to danger and loss and the person in possession has obtained it through fraud or force the Court will interpose by receiver for the security of the property. It would be different where the property is shown to be 'in medio', that is to say, in the enjoyment of no one, as the Court can hardly do wrong in taking possession: it will then be the common interest of all the parties that the Court should prevent a scramble as no one seems to be in actual lawful enjoyment of the property and no harm can be done to anyone by taking it and preserving it for the benefit of the legitimate who may prove successful. Therefore, even if there is no allegation of waste and mismanagement the fact that the property is more or less 'in medio' is sufficient to vest a Court with jurisdiction to appoint a receiver, Otherwise a receiver should not be appointed in supersession of a bone fide possessor of property in controversy and bona fides have to be presumed until the contrary is established or can be indubitably inferred. (5) The Court, on the application of a receiver, looks to the conduct of the party who makes the application and will usually refuse to interfere unless his conduct has been free from blame. He must come to Court with clean hands and should not have disentitled himself to the equitable relief by laches, delay, acquiescence etc.
advocateNotes: Lead precise pleadings on default, user change, subletting, or bona fide need.
Anticipate subsequent-event defenses and address them with evidence.

advocateNotes: Do not presume automatic stay of one proceeding because another is pending.
